sections.heading: Follow-Up
sections.content: Post-therapy follow-up is optimally performed by members of the treatment team and includes the performance of regular history/physical examinations every 4 to 6 months for the first 5 years after primary therapy and annually thereafter. Mammography should be performed annually. For patients treated with BCT and RT, annual mammograms are the appropriate frequency for surveillance, with no clear advantage to shorter interval imaging. Patients should wait 6 to 12 months after the completion of RT to begin their annual mammogram surveillance [BINV-17]. Suspicious findings on physical examination or surveillance imaging should warrant immediate follow-up/workup. The Panel notes that routine imaging of reconstructed breast is not indicated after mastectomy but diagnostic imaging may be required for new symptomatic concerns [BINV-H 5 of 7]. In the absence of clinical signs and symptoms suggestive of recurrent disease, laboratory or imaging studies to screen for metastasis are not necessary. The routine performance of alkaline phosphatase tests and LFTs are not included in the guidelines [429-431]. There is no evidence to support the use of 'tumor markers' for breast cancer, and routine bone scans, CT scans, MRI scans, PET scans, or ultrasound examinations in the asymptomatic patient provide no advantage in survival or ability to palliate recurrent disease [110,432]. Studies indicate that annual mammograms are the appropriate frequency for surveillance of patients with breast cancer who have had BCS and RT with no clear advantage to shorter interval imaging [BINV-17]. The majority of recurrences of DCIS are in-breast recurrences after BCT, and recurrences mostly occur in close proximity to the location of the prior disease. Overall, approximately one-half of the local recurrences after initial treatment for a pure DCIS are invasive in nature, whereas the remainder recur as pure DCIS [MS-9]. For invasive breast cancer, locoregional recurrences can occur at the breast/chest wall, axillary nodes, or supraclavicular/internal mammary nodes. The risk of recurrence between years 5 and 20 is considerable, highlighting the importance of extended endocrine therapy considerations in HR-positive disease [318]. Rates of contralateral breast cancer are increased in patients with BRCA1/2 mutations when compared with patients with sporadic breast cancer [433-435].

timeline.title: First 5 years after primary therapy
timeline.body: Perform regular history and physical examinations every 4 to 6 months. For patients treated with BCT and RT, wait 6 to 12 months after completion of RT to begin annual mammogram surveillance; then annual mammograms thereafter.

timeline.title: After 5 years
timeline.body: Perform annual history and physical examination. Annual mammography continues.

late_effects.name: Cardiotoxicity monitoring
late_effects.detail: Cardiotoxicity monitoring for patients who received left-sided RT, anthracyclines, or HER2-targeted therapy [BINV-17]

late_effects.name: Bone health monitoring
late_effects.detail: Bone health monitoring: BMD determination at baseline and periodically for patients on aromatase inhibitors or with ovarian failure secondary to treatment [BINV-17]

late_effects.name: Lymphedema screening
late_effects.detail: Lymphedema screening and referral as needed [BINV-17]

late_effects.name: Gynecologic screening
late_effects.detail: Age-appropriate gynecologic screening for patients on tamoxifen; routine endometrial biopsy is not recommended but rapid evaluation of vaginal spotting is indicated due to risk of tamoxifen-associated endometrial carcinoma [436]

late_effects.name: Family history and genetic testing
late_effects.detail: Periodic screening for changes in family history and genetic testing indications [BINV-17]

late_effects.name: Cognitive dysfunction screening
late_effects.detail: Cognitive dysfunction screening per NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship [BINV-28]

late_effects.name: Psychosocial support
late_effects.detail: Psychosocial support including screening for anxiety, fear of progression, depression, and PTSD [BINV-17, BINV-28]

late_effects.name: Fertility and sexual health assessment
late_effects.detail: Fertility and sexual health assessment per BINV-C

late_effects.name: Menopausal hormone therapy
late_effects.detail: Menopausal hormone therapy is relatively contraindicated in survivors of hormonally mediated cancers; should be used with caution in those with increased genetic cancer risk [BINV-17]
